architecture_rules.workspace_layout: All Rust crates under crates/<name>/. Workspace root Cargo.toml owns lints + dependency pins. No crate adds its own lints — inherit from workspace.lints.
architecture_rules.crate_naming: All AIOS crates prefixed with aios- (aios-action, aios-evidence, aios-policy, aios-capability-runtime, aios-fs, aios-session).
architecture_rules.schema_first: Each crate that exposes a wire surface MUST have a corresponding proto3 schema in its proto/ subdirectory, matching the rev.2 sub-spec's Appendix A IDL. Code generation via tonic-build at compile time.
architecture_rules.no_unwrap: Workspace lint forbids .unwrap(), .expect(), panic!(), todo!(), unimplemented!(). Use Result<T, E> + thiserror for all error paths.
architecture_rules.no_unsafe: Workspace lint forbids unsafe code. Exceptions require explicit DEC entry.

spec_references.design_decisions: 002.AI-OS.NET--SPECREV.2/02_design_decisions.md (DEC log, newest at top of post-foundation block — DEC-056 currently)

constitutional_invariants_to_uphold: INV-006 Web UI localhost-only by default
INV-009 Approvals bound to one request and expire
INV-011 Cross-group access forbidden by default
INV-012 Recovery required for system mutation
INV-013 AI cannot perform system admin
INV-016 AI cannot grade its own work
INV-018 Vault never leaks raw secrets
INV-019..024 Visual identity / chrome / GPU constitutional

implementation_status_taxonomy.rule: Never claim REAL without explicit evidence at the required grade per S6.1 §10 / S6.2 §6

reusable_lessons.rfc8785_jcs_adoption: RFC 8785 (JSON Canonicalization Scheme) in Rust is a one-dep adoption: `serde_jcs = "0.1"` (Governor-approved in T-006). The crate exposes `serde_jcs::to_string<T: Serialize>(value: &T) -> serde_json::Result<String>`; drop-in replacement for an inline `serde_json::to_string` after a recursive key-sort pass. Pulls in `ryu-js` (the ECMA-262 ryu fork) for §3.2.2.3 number normalization. The deficit being closed: under plain `serde_json::to_string`, `1.5e2` and `150.0` (numerically equal IEEE-754 doubles) emit as different strings and therefore hash to different `request_hash` values — which silently breaks idempotency_key safe-retry. Pin this via a golden test that constructs two requests with `serde_json::Number::from_f64(1.5e2)` vs literal `150.0` and asserts `request_hash` equality.
reusable_lessons.rust_integration_test_layout: Cargo treats every `crates/<crate>/tests/*.rs` as an independent integration target — no Cargo.toml entry needed. Shared helpers go in `tests/common/mod.rs` and are included from each target via `mod common;` (a sub-mod under tests/). Each target compiles to its own binary, so `dead_code` warnings fire when a target uses a subset of helpers — use `#![allow(dead_code)]` at the module level with a `reason = "..."` attribute under `clippy::allow_attributes_without_reason`. Fixture files live in `tests/fixtures/*.json`; resolve absolute paths via `PathBuf::from(env!("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR")).join("tests/fixtures")` rather than relying on cwd.
reusable_lessons.transparent_serde_id_newtypes_skip_valid: An ID newtype declared `#[serde(transparent)]` over a `String` will accept ANY string in JSON deserialization — its `parse()` validator does NOT run. This is the correct Wave-11 posture (validation belongs at construction-time, not at the wire), but it means golden fixtures can carry opaque test-only id strings (`act_01HQZX1234PARENT00000ABCDE`) without needing real ULIDs.
reusable_lessons.clippy_type_complexity_in_tests: `[(fn(&mut T), U); N]` arrays of function pointers trip `clippy::type_complexity` even inside `#[test]` functions. The cheapest fix is a local type alias: `type WalkerFn = fn(&mut ActionEnvelope);` followed by `let walkers: [(WalkerFn, ActionPhase); 3] = ...`. Avoid `#[allow(clippy::type_complexity)]` for one-off tuples — the alias documents intent better and survives the next refactor.
reusable_lessons.clippy_doc_markdown_for_serde_json: Every PascalCase identifier in a doc comment must be backticked under `clippy::doc_markdown`, including casual mentions like `serde_json` (no underscore!) and `Number::F64`. Spec terms in normal prose (`RFC 8785`, `JCS`, `ECMA-262`) do NOT trigger it; the rule fires on identifier-shaped words only.
reusable_lessons.fixture_to_envelope_canonical_check: When validating that a hand-written fixture's structure matches the Rust envelope shape, do a *both-paths* canonical comparison: (1) parse fixture as `serde_json::Value`, JCS-canonicalize → `raw_canon`; (2) parse fixture as `ActionEnvelope`, JCS-canonicalize → `env_canon`; assert `raw_canon == env_canon`. If a fixture field is unknown to the struct, serde silently drops it, the env_canon string will be shorter, and the assertion will fail with a clear diff. This is cheaper than maintaining a denylist of fields.
reusable_lessons.type_level_append_only_invariant_pattern: To encode INV-005 (append-only) at the Rust type level for an immutable record value, combine FOUR mechanisms: (1) ALL struct fields private (no `pub field`), (2) ONLY `&self` accessors — no `&mut self` method anywhere on the public API, (3) construction routed exclusively through a consuming Builder.seal(self) -> Result<T, E> that yields the immutable value, (4) a Sealed marker trait whose super-trait is in a private module so downstream crates cannot impl it. Deserialize can still be derived (receipts must be loadable from disk) — but the consumer's append/verify_integrity re-validates the link hash before admitting the receipt, so there is no edit-and-re-serialize attack path in safe Rust. The Sealed trait is the most subtle bit: `mod private { pub trait SealedSuper {} }` + `pub trait Sealed: private::SealedSuper {}` blocks downstream impl because external crates cannot name `private::SealedSuper`. Used in aios-evidence (T-007).
reusable_lessons.cross_crate_workspace_dependency: Adding a second workspace crate that depends on the first: in the consumer's `crates/<consumer>/Cargo.toml` use `<producer> = { path = "../<producer>" }` — no version, no registry. Workspace inheritance still works for other deps via `{ workspace = true }`. The producer's `pub use` re-export surface is what the consumer sees; bring re-exports up to `lib.rs` early (the consumer should NOT have to write `aios_action::canonical::jcs_canonicalize` — it should write `aios_action::jcs_canonicalize` thanks to the producer's re-export).
reusable_lessons.closed_enum_serde_rename_per_variant: For a closed enum whose wire form is the exact spec string (SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE in S3.1, PascalCase in S0.1 §7.3), prefer per-variant `#[serde(rename = "EXACT_STRING")]` over `#[serde(rename_all = ...)]`. Reasons: (1) drift between Rust identifier and spec wire-name becomes impossible without touching this file, (2) variants whose Rust-camelCase would NOT correspond to the spec under any rename_all rule (e.g. `E2ePassed` -> `E2E_PASSED`, where rename_all=SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE would emit `E2E_PASSED` but the camelCase capitalisation of `2e` is brittle) are pinned explicitly. The cost is one line per variant; the win is a single source of truth that the spec audit can grep for.
reusable_lessons.sign_canonical_minus_signature_pattern: When a struct carries its own signature, signing the JCS bytes of the whole struct produces a chicken-and-egg cycle (the signature is part of the bytes being signed). Resolution: a private `ForSigning<'a>` projection mirrors the public struct field-for-field EXCEPT the signature field; `From<&PublicStruct>` builds the projection by reference. Both `seal_signed` (producer) and `verify_signature` (consumer) canonicalize this projection, BLAKE3-256 it, and sign/verify over the resulting hex digest bytes. Signing the *hex digest bytes* (not the raw 32-byte hash) makes the signed message deterministic and debuggable across re-serializations — and matches the verifier path symmetrically. Used in aios-evidence T-009 for the Ed25519 receipt-signing path.
reusable_lessons.ed25519_dalek_2_1_api: ed25519-dalek 2.1.x (BSD-3-Clause, dalek-cryptography org) — pure-Rust Ed25519. Key types: SigningKey (32 bytes), VerifyingKey (32 bytes), Signature (64 bytes, fixed). Construction: `SigningKey::from_bytes(&[u8; 32])` is infallible (any 32 bytes is a valid seed); `SigningKey::generate(&mut OsRng)` with rand_core 0.6 feature `getrandom`. Signing: import `ed25519_dalek::Signer` trait, call `signing_key.sign(msg)` -> Signature; signing is infallible for pure Ed25519. Verification: import `ed25519_dalek::Verifier` trait, call `verifying_key.verify(msg, &sig)` -> Result<(), SignatureError>. `verify_strict` rejects malleable signatures; `verify` accepts them — use `verify_strict` only if the cryptographic threat model demands it. Signature::from_bytes(&[u8; 64]) is infallible (returns Signature, NOT Result, in 2.1). Features: `std` is default; `rand_core` exposes the keygen path. License BSD-3-Clause is permissive (attribution-only) and compatible with workspace Apache-2.0. Used in aios-evidence T-009.
reusable_lessons.hex_codec_without_dep: For lowercase-hex encode/decode of fixed-size byte arrays in a workspace that doesn't pull in `hex` (or wants the codec inlined for clarity / lint reasons): encode via `for byte in bytes { write!(&mut out, "{byte:02x}")?; }` — `core::fmt::Write` is in scope after `use core::fmt::Write as _;`. Writes to String are infallible but the trait API returns Result, so discard via `let _ = write!(...);` to satisfy `unused_must_use` without unwrap. Decode by `chunks_exact(2)` and a small `hex_nibble(u8) -> Result<u8, E>` matching `b'0'..=b'9' | b'a'..=b'f'` — explicitly rejecting uppercase preserves the canonical-lowercase invariant from S0.1 §8.5 / S3.1 §5.4. Total cost: ~30 lines, zero deps, two new error variants. Used in aios-evidence T-009 encode/decode_signature_hex.
reusable_lessons.consuming_seal_pattern_for_segments: Sealing an in-memory container into an immutable terminal value uses the same consuming-builder pattern as ReceiptBuilder.seal: `Segment::seal(self, prev_id, prev_hash, &SigningKey) -> Result<SealedSegment, _>`. By moving `self`, the open Segment is dropped after seal returns — no &mut self mutation needed and the closed `is_sealed` flag is purely defensive against future internal-mutation code paths. The terminal SEGMENT_SEALED receipt is appended inside seal *before* the seal hash is computed so the receipt count carried in the seal metadata matches the receipt count on the resulting SealedSegment (both include the terminal). The canonical-all-receipts hash, however, is computed over the receipts BEFORE the terminal is appended (the terminal is the witness; it cannot witness itself). Used in aios-evidence T-010.
reusable_lessons.rfc3339_in_blake3_seed: S3.1 §7.1 derives segment_id from BLAKE3(genesis_receipt_id + sealed_at_timestamp). The canonical timestamp encoding for AIOS is RFC 3339 UTC (chrono::DateTime<Utc>::to_rfc3339()), NOT the i64 nanosecond representation. Reason: RFC 3339 is the wire format on every other surface in S0.1 / S3.1, so anchoring the segment id seed to the same encoding keeps the id reproducible from a snapshot exporter that only sees the serialized envelopes. Used in aios-evidence T-010 Segment::seal.
reusable_lessons.cross_chain_genesis_64_zero_hex: When a chain carries cross-segment linkage via a previous-hash field (S3.1 §5.2 line 193 says 'The first segment's value is \"0000...0000\"'), expose the zero-hex string as a `pub const GENESIS_PREVIOUS_SEGMENT_SEAL_HASH: &str = \"00...00\";` from the relevant module. Two reasons: (1) call sites compare against a named constant rather than a string literal — code review can spot drift; (2) the SegmentChain's verify_chain pass needs the same constant when distinguishing a malformed genesis (carries a non-zero, non-None previous hash) from a healthy one (carries None or the constant). Used in aios-evidence T-010.
reusable_lessons.vendored_protoc_for_grpc_crates: Every AIOS gRPC crate needs `protoc` at build time (tonic-build shells out to it for proto parsing). The AIOS dev box has NO system protoc. Solution: add `protoc-bin-vendored = "3.0"` as a build-dep, then in `build.rs` set `std::env::set_var("PROTOC", protoc_bin_vendored::protoc_bin_path()?)` BEFORE calling `tonic_build::configure().compile_protos(...)`. This pulls a pre-built protoc binary into the target dir at build time only — zero runtime cost, removes the system-install dependency for every future AIOS gRPC crate (capability runtime, policy kernel, vault broker). Add `protoc-bin-vendored = "3.0"` to `[workspace.dependencies]`. License is the Google protobuf BSD-3-Clause+Apache-2.0+MIT mix, compatible with workspace Apache-2.0. Used in aios-evidence T-011 as the template build.rs pattern.
reusable_lessons.tonic_0_12_service_module_pattern: Structure a tonic gRPC service crate with FOUR files under src/service/: (1) `mod.rs` hosts `tonic::include_proto!("<pkg>")` inside `pub mod proto { #[allow(clippy::all, clippy::pedantic, clippy::nursery, clippy::cargo, missing_docs, ...)] }` (broad allow because prost emits many pedantic-flagged patterns) + flat re-exports (EvidenceLogService, EvidenceLogServer, EvidenceLogClient); (2) `conversions.rs` is the SINGLE Rust↔proto bridge — keep all proto type construction here so the rest of the crate never touches the generated module directly; (3) `impl_inmemory.rs` is the reference backend implementing the generated `EvidenceLog` trait (use `#[async_trait]` per tonic 0.12's macro expansion contract); (4) `server.rs` exposes a thin `build_router(backend) -> tonic::transport::server::Router` + an optional `serve(backend, addr)` wrapper — no built-in TLS bootstrap (production binaries chain their own tls_config). Apply `#[allow(clippy::result_large_err, reason = "tonic::Status is canonical")]` to the trait impl block — `Result<_, tonic::Status>` triggers `result_large_err` because Status is 176 bytes, and the lint cannot be satisfied without breaking the generated surface. Used in aios-evidence T-011 as the template for every future AIOS service crate.
reusable_lessons.tonic_grpc_stack_lock: Per §21 stack lock, AIOS gRPC services use: tonic 0.12 (transport + tls features), tonic-build 0.12, prost 0.13, prost-types 0.13, tokio 1 (full feature set), tokio-stream 0.1 with explicit `sync` feature (REQUIRED for BroadcastStream which powers server-streaming RPCs backed by tokio::sync::broadcast), async-trait 0.1, protoc-bin-vendored 3.0 (build-only). All pre-approved by Governor in T-011. Workspace `[workspace.dependencies]` carries them; per-crate `[dependencies]` + `[build-dependencies]` reference via `{ workspace = true }`. Streaming RPC servers use `type FooStream = Pin<Box<dyn Stream<Item = Result<T, Status>> + Send + 'static>>`; live fan-out via `tokio::sync::broadcast::Sender<T>` (capacity ~1024) and `BroadcastStream::new(rx).filter_map(...)`. Integration tests bind to `127.0.0.1:0` (random port), spawn the server with `serve_with_shutdown` + oneshot cancellation channel for clean test teardown.
reusable_lessons.rust_to_proto_enum_bridge_via_wire_name: When a hand-written closed Rust enum (with `#[serde(rename = ...)]` per variant) must round-trip to a prost-generated proto enum (with `as_str_name()` / `from_str_name()` accessors), the simplest sustainable bridge is the SHARED SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE wire name as the lookup key. Expose `as_wire_str(self) -> &'static str` on the hand-written enum (mirrors the serde rename), then convert via `proto::Enum::from_str_name(rt.as_wire_str())`. For the inverse, iterate the hand-written enum via `strum::EnumIter` and find by wire-name match. Pin the cross-walk by a strum-iter round-trip test: `for rt in HandWritten::iter() { assert_eq!(proto::Enum::from_str_name(rt.as_wire_str()).unwrap().as_str_name(), rt.as_wire_str()) }`. With 427+ variants this scales linearly in compile time and constant in runtime (the test is the only place that iterates). Used in aios-evidence T-011 for the RecordType bridge.
reusable_lessons.wave_carry_forward_in_proto_envelope: When a Rust value type is a strict SUBSET of the wire proto envelope (e.g. T-007's 10-field EvidenceReceipt vs S3.1 §3's 17-field proto EvidenceReceipt), document the carry-forward fields IN the conversions module's table at the top, populate them with proto3 defaults (empty string, 0, false, None) in the `to_proto` projection, and reference the deferring spec section (e.g. §22.4 / Wave 14) in a comment per field. Reasoning: future-Wave reconciliation extends the Rust type without breaking the wire format — the conversions module is the single touch point. Mark each defaulted field with `// §22.4 carry-forward — populated by Wave 14+ as the contract extends.` so audit can grep for the carry-forward stance. Used in aios-evidence T-011 for the 7 carry-forward EvidenceReceipt fields (policy_decision_id, verification_id, correlation_id, trace_id, segment_id, sequence_number, payload_ref, redaction_profile, simulated, payload one-of).
reusable_lessons.evidence_error_to_tonic_status_mapping: Translate a domain error enum to `tonic::Status` via `impl From<DomainError> for tonic::Status` with an explicit `match` over every variant. Mapping policy: DataLoss for chain/signature integrity failures (these are constitutional tamper indicators per §28.5 — client MUST NOT retry); InvalidArgument for malformed inputs (id, subject, signature hex); FailedPrecondition for state-mismatch (missing signature on verify, empty chain, sealed segment); Internal for encoding/hash mismatches that indicate a server-side bug. Always include the full `err.to_string()` as the Status message — gRPC clients depend on it for diagnostics. Avoid `Unknown` as a catch-all if possible: explicit per-variant matching makes future error additions a compile-time review point. Used in aios-evidence T-011 for the 16-variant EvidenceError to tonic::Status mapping.
reusable_lessons.redaction_before_hash_pipeline: For a content-addressed log with privacy guarantees, redaction MUST happen BEFORE the content hash and signature are computed — the cryptographic chain witnesses the redacted form, not the raw form. Pipeline: raw_payload → apply_redaction(profile, record_type, payload) → redacted_payload → JCS canonicalize → BLAKE3 content_hash → Ed25519 signature over BLAKE3(JCS(receipt-minus-signature)). The redaction_profile is stored on the receipt AND included in the signing projection so post-seal profile relabeling breaks signature verification. Back-compat with pre-redaction receipt JSON uses #[serde(default)] on the new field. The constitutional anchor (S3.1 §14): there is no 'unredacted-but-signed' representation anywhere — the raw payload is never persisted. Used in aios-evidence T-013.
reusable_lessons.per_record_type_policy_via_prefix_catego: When a closed enum has hundreds of variants and per-variant policy is needed (e.g. 427 RecordType variants × 3 redaction profiles = 1281 cells), do NOT write a 427-arm match. Instead, exploit the spec's own namespace coding: enum variants whose wire names share a prefix (VAULT_*, RECOVERY_*, TELEMETRY_*) share policy. Build a coarse category enum (~6 values) and a single `category_for(variant) -> Category` function that prefix-matches the variant's `as_wire_str()` output. Then write per-(category, profile) policy in compact form. Adding a new variant requires assigning a category (or no change if it falls into Generic), not authoring a fresh per-variant rule. The Generic catch-all defaults to the most-aggressive profile so unclassified variants fail closed. Used in aios-evidence T-013 for the RecordType → RecordCategory routing under the 3 RedactionProfile values.
reusable_lessons.normalize_key_for_robust_pattern_matchin: When matching JSON object keys against secret/PII patterns (password, api_key, token, ...), case-insensitive substring isn't enough. Real payloads use hyphens (`x-api-key`, `Content-Type`), dots (`config.password`), and camelCase (`clientSecret`). Solution: a `normalize_key(k) -> String` that lowercases AND converts hyphens/spaces/dots to underscore AND inserts underscore at camelCase boundaries. Then pattern list contains only canonical lowercase-with-underscore forms (`api_key`, `client_secret`) and matches via substring on the normalized key. Test coverage: header-style names, camelCase variants, all-uppercase legacy names — one test per shape locks in the normalization contract. Used in aios-evidence T-013 redact_keys + hash_pii_keys.
reusable_lessons.structural_marker_preserves_correlation_: Aggressive redaction profiles (e.g. Strict reducing payload bodies to ~zero content) still need to preserve evidence-chain correlation. The structural marker pattern: replace the payload with a fixed 3-field object {record_type: <wire name>, payload_hash_prefix: <BLAKE3-truncated 32 hex over the stripped pre-reduction canonical bytes>, redacted_under_profile: <profile name>}. Two receipts with identical raw bodies still produce the same payload_hash_prefix — auditors can correlate equal payloads across receipts without seeing what was in them. Different bodies yield different prefixes. The marker itself is byte-stable so the receipt's content_hash is well-defined. Used in aios-evidence T-013 reduce_to_structural_marker (Strict + Lifecycle/Telemetry/Generic categories).
reusable_lessons.tonic_streaming_trailers_workaround: tonic 0.12 server-streaming has no clean API to attach trailing metadata on the success path (Status::with_metadata sets trailers, but only on the final ending Status — which requires breaking the stream-from-vec pattern and emitting the status manually). When a spec mandates a trailer count (S3.1 §10 'count returned in the stream trailer'), the pragmatic compromise is to emit it via initial-metadata header (`response.metadata_mut().insert(KEY, value)`). Clients read header and trailer identically via `Response::metadata()`, so the observable contract is preserved. Document the choice in a helper (e.g. attach_suppressed_count()) and reserve the trailer migration for when the implementation switches to streaming-from-disk. Used in aios-evidence T-014 for `x-aios-suppressed-count`.
reusable_lessons.proto3_additive_field_extension_pattern: When extending an existing spec-declared proto message with new wire-required fields, proto3 makes ADDITIVE extensions backward-compatible: older clients/servers ignore unknown fields, all new fields take proto3 default values (empty string, 0, false, None) automatically. Strategy: keep all existing fields with their existing tag numbers; allocate new fields starting from the next unused tag; document each new field with the spec ref + the carry-forward stance ('older callers ignore; production callers MUST populate'). Existing test fixtures that build the proto struct literally must be updated to set the new fields to their defaults (proto-generated types do impl Default but proto3 struct-literal construction is exhaustive in Rust). Used in aios-evidence T-014 for QueryRequest/SubscribeRequest +caller_* fields.
reusable_lessons.wire_form_predicate_for_broadcast_stream: When a per-event filter predicate normally operates on a sealed in-memory value (`predicate(&SealedReceipt) -> bool`), but must also filter live broadcast events where only the wire-form proto is at hand, do NOT acquire the async RwLock inside the broadcast `filter_map` closure to resurrect the sealed value (it's hostile to filter_map's signature and serializes all event delivery on the lock). Instead, extract the inputs that matter (e.g. subject canonical id + record type) and write a wire-form variant `predicate_wire(subject: &str, record_type: RecordType) -> bool` that operates on the wire projection. Have the sealed-form `predicate(&SealedReceipt)` delegate to the wire-form. This keeps live broadcast O(1) per event with no lock contention. Used in aios-evidence T-014 for PrivacyCeiling::admits_wire().
reusable_lessons.sync_trait_over_tokio_locked_state: When a synchronous trait (e.g. CompactionBackend) must operate over state guarded by `tokio::sync::RwLock`, bridge via `RwLock::blocking_write()`/`blocking_read()`. These panic if called inside a tokio runtime task — production wraps the call in `tokio::task::spawn_blocking`. The integration test pattern: spawn_blocking the worker tick, .await the JoinHandle, propagate Result. Why not just make the trait async? Because the worker logic is pure single-threaded sequencing (walk segments, decide policy, request compaction) and a sync trait keeps it trivially testable with a fake backend — the async-awkwardness is confined to the production backends' adapter methods. Used in aios-evidence T-015 CompactionBackend impls for InMemoryEvidenceLog and RocksDbEvidenceLog.
reusable_lessons.compaction_state_in_separate_metadata_cf: When a sealed-segment store needs compaction state (compacted_at timestamp) but the segment row's byte layout is the input to cryptographic verification on reopen, do NOT add compaction state to the segment row. Use a separate metadata-CF sentinel key `compacted_at::<segment_id>`. Reasons: (1) the segment row remains byte-identical to its pre-compaction form, so recover_sealed_chain's `serde_json::from_slice::<SealedSegment>` keeps round-tripping; (2) operators can scan the metadata CF for `compacted_at::*` prefix to enumerate compacted segments without loading every segment; (3) the schema migration if compaction state grows (e.g. compacted_by, compacted_size) is contained to the metadata CF. Used in aios-evidence T-015 RocksDbEvidenceLog::compact_segment.
reusable_lessons.filter_already_compacted_from_eligibilit: A compaction worker that re-evaluates eligibility on every tick will re-discover already-compacted segments (since compact_segment preserves the segment metadata as a tombstone per §12). Under OperatorApproval mode this drives the worker to re-emit COMPACTION_APPROVAL_REQUIRED records every tick — operator UX disaster + chain pollution. Fix: the backend's `list_sealed_segments` MUST filter out already-compacted segments. The in-memory predicate: warm_receipt_count > 0. The RocksDB predicate: `rocksdb_segment_compacted_at(db, &seg_id).is_none()`. The worker stays oblivious to compaction state; the backend owns the predicate. Used in aios-evidence T-015.
reusable_lessons.wave_14_plus_record_type_extension_patte: When the spec freezes a closed enum vocabulary (e.g. Wave 13 RecordType 1..=427) AND reserves a forward-growth range (1000..=9999), adding ONE new variant after closure requires careful test pinning. Pattern: (1) declare the new variant with a comment block citing the constitutional source; (2) extend `as_wire_str`, `retention_class`, `wire_id` match arms; (3) update the proto IDL — `COMPACTION_APPROVAL_REQUIRED = 1000` THEN `reserved 1001 to 9999` (proto3 forbids defining a value at a reserved id); (4) split the existing density test into TWO: `wave_13_id_range_is_contiguous_and_dense` (asserts 1..=427 stays dense) + `wave_N_plus_ids_live_in_the_reserved_block` (asserts new ids are in 1000..=9999); (5) introduce TOTAL_RECORD_TYPE_VARIANTS = WAVE_13_VARIANTS + WAVE_N_PLUS_VARIANTS as the test-level total. The split-test pattern is the constitutional anchor for every future Wave N+ addition. Used in aios-evidence T-015 for CompactionApprovalRequired (ID 1000, FOREVER).
reusable_lessons.milestone_crate_version_bump_on_closure: When a workspace crate reaches a milestone closure (M1, M2, ...), bump its Cargo.toml version OUT of `version.workspace = true` (which inherits the global 0.0.1) to a literal `version = "0.X.0"` matching the milestone parity. Document the choice in a comment block (milestone scope + API stability statement). This signals milestone parity without forcing every other in-tree crate (still pre-milestone in many cases) into a coordinated bump. The crate still inherits edition / rust-version / license / repository / authors from workspace — only the version field is overridden. The API is NOT yet semver-stable; the bump signals 'feature-complete for milestone M', not '1.0 stable'. Used in aios-evidence T-015 / M2 closure: version 0.0.1 → 0.1.0.
